[Chicago Fire FC](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_FC "Chicago Fire FC") is a member of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS) and plays at Soldier Field. The Fire have won one league title and four [U.S. Open Cups](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt_U.S._Open_Cup "Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cup"), since their founding in 1997. In 1994, the United States hosted a successful [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup") with games played at Soldier Field.[[285]](#cite_note-291)

The [Chicago Stars FC](/wiki/Chicago_Stars_FC "Chicago Stars FC") are a team in the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") (NWSL). They previously played in [Women's Professional Soccer](/wiki/Women%27s_Professional_Soccer "Women's Professional Soccer") (WPS), of which they were a founding member, before joining the NWSL in 2013. They play at [SeatGeek Stadium](/wiki/SeatGeek_Stadium "SeatGeek Stadium") in [Bridgeview, Illinois](/wiki/Bridgeview,_Illinois "Bridgeview, Illinois").

The [Chicago Sky](/wiki/Chicago_Sky "Chicago Sky") is a professional basketball team playing in the [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") (WNBA). They play home games at the [Wintrust Arena](/wiki/Wintrust_Arena "Wintrust Arena"). The team was founded before the 2006 WNBA season began.[[286]](#cite_note-292)

The [Chicago Marathon](/wiki/Chicago_Marathon "Chicago Marathon") has been held each year since 1977 except for 1987, when a half marathon was run in its place. The Chicago Marathon is one of six [World Marathon Majors](/wiki/World_Marathon_Majors "World Marathon Majors").[[287]](#cite_note-293)

Five area colleges play in [Division I](/wiki/Division_I_(NCAA) "Division I (NCAA)") conferences: two from major conferences—the [DePaul Blue Demons](/wiki/DePaul_Blue_Demons "DePaul Blue Demons") ([Big East Conference](/wiki/Big_East_Conference "Big East Conference")) and the [Northwestern Wildcats](/wiki/Northwestern_Wildcats "Northwestern Wildcats") ([Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"))—and three from other D1 conferences—the [Chicago State Cougars](/wiki/Chicago_State_Cougars "Chicago State Cougars") ([Northeast Conference](/wiki/Northeast_Conference "Northeast Conference")); the [Loyola Ramblers](/wiki/Loyola_Ramblers "Loyola Ramblers") ([Atlantic 10 Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_10_Conference "Atlantic 10 Conference")); and the [UIC Flames](/wiki/UIC_Flames "UIC Flames") ([Missouri Valley Conference](/wiki/Missouri_Valley_Conference "Missouri Valley Conference")).[[288]](#cite_note-294)

Chicago has also entered into [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports") with the creation of the [OpTic Chicago](/wiki/OpTic_Chicago "OpTic Chicago"), a professional [Call of Duty](/wiki/Call_of_Duty "Call of Duty") team that participates within the [CDL](/wiki/Call_of_Duty_League "Call of Duty League").[[289]](#cite_note-295)

## Parks and greenspace

Main articles: [Parks in Chicago](/wiki/Parks_in_Chicago "Parks in Chicago"), [Chicago Boulevard System](/wiki/Chicago_Boulevard_System "Chicago Boulevard System"), and [Cook County Forest Preserves](/wiki/Cook_County_Forest_Preserves "Cook County Forest Preserves")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Buckingham_Fountain_in_Chicago_at_night.jpg/250px-Buckingham_Fountain_in_Chicago_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buckingham_Fountain_in_Chicago_at_night.jpg)

[Buckingham Fountain](/wiki/Buckingham_Fountain "Buckingham Fountain") is in [Grant Park](/wiki/Grant_Park_(Chicago) "Grant Park (Chicago)") in the [Loop](/wiki/Chicago_Loop "Chicago Loop").

When Chicago was incorporated in 1837, it chose the motto *Urbs in Horto*, a [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") phrase which means "City in a Garden". Today, the [Chicago Park District](/wiki/Chicago_Park_District "Chicago Park District") consists of more than 570 parks with over 8,000 acres (3,200 ha) of [municipal parkland](/wiki/Municipal_park "Municipal park"). There are 31 sand [beaches](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_Chicago,_Illinois "List of beaches in Chicago, Illinois"), a plethora of museums, two world-class conservatories, and 50 nature areas.[[290]](#cite_note-296) Lincoln Park, the largest of the city's parks, covers 1,200 acres (490 ha) and has over 20 million visitors each year, making it third in the number of visitors after [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), and the [National Mall and Memorial Parks](/wiki/National_Mall_and_Memorial_Parks "National Mall and Memorial Parks") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[291]](#cite_note-297)

There is a historic [boulevard system](/wiki/Chicago_boulevard_system "Chicago boulevard system"),[[292]](#cite_note-298) a network of wide, tree-lined [boulevards](/wiki/Boulevard "Boulevard") which connect a number of Chicago [parks](/wiki/Parks_in_Chicago "Parks in Chicago").[[293]](#cite_note-WTTW-299) The boulevards and the parks were authorized by the Illinois legislature in 1869.[[294]](#cite_note-300) A number of [Chicago neighborhoods](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Chicago "Neighborhoods of Chicago") emerged along these roadways in the 19th century.[[293]](#cite_note-WTTW-299) The building of the boulevard system continued intermittently until 1942. It includes nineteen boulevards, eight parks, and six [squares](/wiki/Town_square "Town square"), along twenty-six miles of interconnected streets.[[295]](#cite_note-301) The *Chicago Park Boulevard System Historic District* was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2018.[[296]](#cite_note-weekly-302)[[297]](#cite_note-NR2018-303)

With berths for more than 6,000 boats, the Chicago Park District operates the nation's largest municipal harbor system.[[298]](#cite_note-304) In addition to ongoing beautification and renewal projects for the existing parks, a number of new parks have been added in recent years, such as the [Ping Tom Memorial Park](/wiki/Ping_Tom_Memorial_Park "Ping Tom Memorial Park") in Chinatown, [DuSable Park](/wiki/DuSable_Park_(Chicago) "DuSable Park (Chicago)") on the Near North Side, and most notably, Millennium Park, which is in the northwestern corner of one of Chicago's oldest parks, Grant Park in the Chicago Loop.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The wealth of greenspace afforded by Chicago's parks is further augmented by the [Cook County Forest Preserves](/wiki/Cook_County_Forest_Preserves "Cook County Forest Preserves"), a network of open spaces containing forest, [prairie](/wiki/Prairie "Prairie"), [wetland](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland"), streams, and lakes that are set aside as natural areas which lie along the city's outskirts,[[299]](#cite_note-305) including both the [Chicago Botanic Garden](/wiki/Chicago_Botanic_Garden "Chicago Botanic Garden") in [Glencoe](/wiki/Glencoe,_Illinois "Glencoe, Illinois") and the [Brookfield Zoo](/wiki/Brookfield_Zoo "Brookfield Zoo") in [Brookfield](/wiki/Brookfield,_Illinois "Brookfield, Illinois").[[300]](#cite_note-306) Washington Park is also one of the city's biggest parks; covering nearly 400 acres (160 ha). The park is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places listings in South Side Chicago](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_South_Side_Chicago "National Register of Historic Places listings in South Side Chicago").[[301]](#cite_note-307)

## Law and government

### Government

Main article: [Government of Chicago](/wiki/Government_of_Chicago "Government of Chicago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Daley_Plaza_060716.jpg/250px-Daley_Plaza_060716.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daley_Plaza_060716.jpg)

[Daley Plaza](/wiki/Daley_Plaza "Daley Plaza") and the [Chicago Picasso](/wiki/Chicago_Picasso "Chicago Picasso"), with [City Hall-County Building](/wiki/City_Hall-County_Building_(Chicago) "City Hall-County Building (Chicago)") visible in background. At right, the [Daley Center](/wiki/Richard_J._Daley_Center "Richard J. Daley Center") contains the state law courts.

The government of the City of Chicago is divided into executive and [legislative](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") branches. The [mayor of Chicago](/wiki/Mayor_of_Chicago "Mayor of Chicago") is the chief executive, elected by general election for a term of four years, with no term limits. The incumbent mayor is [Brandon Johnson](/wiki/Brandon_Johnson "Brandon Johnson"). The mayor appoints commissioners and other officials who oversee the various departments. As well as the mayor, Chicago's clerk and treasurer are also elected citywide. The [City Council](/wiki/Chicago_City_Council "Chicago City Council") is the legislative branch and is made up of 50 [alderpersons](/wiki/Alderman#United_States "Alderman"), one elected from each [ward](/wiki/Wards_of_the_United_States "Wards of the United States") in the city.[[302]](#cite_note-308) The council takes official action through the passage of [ordinances](/wiki/Local_ordinance "Local ordinance") and resolutions and approves the city budget.[[303]](#cite_note-309)

The [Chicago Police Department](/wiki/Chicago_Police_Department "Chicago Police Department") provides law enforcement and the [Chicago Fire Department](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_Department "Chicago Fire Department") provides fire suppression and emergency medical services for the city and its residents. Civil and criminal law cases are heard in the [Cook County Circuit Court](/wiki/Cook_County_Circuit_Court "Cook County Circuit Court") of the State of Illinois court system, or in the [Northern District of Illinois](/wiki/Northern_District_of_Illinois "Northern District of Illinois"), in the federal system. In the state court, the public prosecutor is the Illinois [state's attorney](/wiki/State%27s_attorney "State's attorney"); in the Federal court it is the United States [attorney](/wiki/District_Attorney "District Attorney").

### Politics

Main article: [Political history of Chicago](/wiki/Political_history_of_Chicago "Political history of Chicago")

Presidential election results in Chicago[[304]](#cite_note-Dave's-310)

| Year | [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") | [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") | Others |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **[2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Illinois "2024 United States presidential election in Illinois")** | **78.3%** *775,699* | 20.6% *203,817* | 1.2% *11,776* |
| **[2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_Illinois "2020 United States presidential election in Illinois")** | **82.5%** *944,735* | 15.8% *181,234* | 1.6% *18,772* |
| **[2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Illinois "2016 United States presidential election in Illinois")** | **82.9%** *912,945* | 12.3% *135,320* | 4.8% *53,262* |

During much of the last half of the 19th century, Chicago's politics were dominated by a growing [Democratic Party](/wiki/Cook_County_Democratic_Party "Cook County Democratic Party") organization. During the 1880s and 1890s, Chicago had a powerful radical tradition with large and highly organized [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist"), [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchist "Anarchist") and [labor organizations](/wiki/Labor_organization "Labor organization").[[305]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchneirov1998173–174-311) For much of the 20th century, Chicago has been among the largest and most reliable Democratic strongholds in the United States; with Chicago's Democratic vote the state of Illinois has been "[solid blue](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states")" in [presidential elections](/wiki/United_States_presidential_election "United States presidential election") since 1992. Even before then, it was not unheard of for Republican presidential candidates to win handily in downstate Illinois, only to lose statewide due to large Democratic margins in Chicago.

The citizens of Chicago have not elected a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") mayor since 1927, when [William Thompson](/wiki/William_Hale_Thompson "William Hale Thompson") was voted into office. The strength of the party in Chicago is partly a consequence of Illinois state politics, where the Republicans have come to represent rural and farm concerns while the Democrats support urban issues such as Chicago's public school funding.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Chicago contains less than 25% of the state's population, but it is split between eight of Illinois' 17 [districts](/wiki/Illinois%27s_congressional_districts "Illinois's congressional districts") in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"). All eight of the city's representatives are Democrats; only two Republicans have represented a significant portion of the city since 1973, for one term each: [Robert P. Hanrahan](/wiki/Robert_P._Hanrahan "Robert P. Hanrahan") from 1973 to 1975, and [Michael Patrick Flanagan](/wiki/Michael_Patrick_Flanagan "Michael Patrick Flanagan") from 1995 to 1997.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Machine politics](/wiki/Machine_politics "Machine politics") persisted in Chicago after the decline of similar machines in other large U.S. cities.[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMontejano199933–34-312) During much of that time, the city administration found opposition mainly from a liberal "independent" faction of the Democratic Party. The independents finally gained control of city government in 1983 with the election of [Harold Washington](/wiki/Harold_Washington "Harold Washington") (in office 1983–1987). From 1989 until May 16, 2011, Chicago was under the leadership of its longest-serving mayor, [Richard M. Daley](/wiki/Richard_M._Daley "Richard M. Daley"), the son of Richard J. Daley. Because of the dominance of the Democratic Party in Chicago, the Democratic [primary](/wiki/Partisan_primary "Partisan primary") vote held in the spring is generally more significant than the general elections in November for U.S. House and Illinois State seats. The aldermanic, mayoral, and other city offices are filled through nonpartisan elections with runoffs as needed.[[307]](#cite_note-313)

The city is home of former United States President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and First Lady [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama"); Barack Obama was formerly a state legislator representing Chicago and later a U.S. senator. The Obamas' residence is located near the University of Chicago in [Kenwood](/wiki/Kenwood,_Chicago "Kenwood, Chicago") on the city's south side.[[308]](#cite_note-314)

### Crime

Main article: [Crime in Chicago](/wiki/Crime_in_Chicago "Crime in Chicago")

See also: [Timeline of organized crime in Chicago](/wiki/Timeline_of_organized_crime_in_Chicago "Timeline of organized crime in Chicago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Chicago_Police_Ford_Police_Interceptor_Utility_7905_%28Front_left_view%29_b.jpg/250px-Chicago_Police_Ford_Police_Interceptor_Utility_7905_%28Front_left_view%29_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_Police_Ford_Police_Interceptor_Utility_7905_(Front_left_view)_b.jpg)

A [Ford Explorer](/wiki/Ford_Explorer "Ford Explorer") [SUV](/wiki/SUV "SUV") as a [Chicago Police Department](/wiki/Chicago_Police_Department "Chicago Police Department") vehicle, 2021

Chicago's crime rate in 2020 was 3,926 per 100,000 people.[[309]](#cite_note-315) Chicago experienced major rises in [violent crime](/wiki/Violent_crime "Violent crime") in the 1920s, in the late 1960s, and in the 2020s.[[310]](#cite_note-316)[[311]](#cite_note-Chicago_Police_Annual_Report_2017-317) Chicago's biggest [criminal justice](/wiki/Criminal_justice "Criminal justice") challenges have changed little over the last 50 years, and statistically reside with homicide, [armed robbery](/wiki/Armed_robbery "Armed robbery"), gang violence, and [aggravated battery](/wiki/Aggravated_battery "Aggravated battery"). Chicago has a higher murder rate than the larger cities of New York and Los Angeles. However, while it has a large absolute number of crimes due to its size, Chicago is not among the top-25 most violent cities in the United States.[[312]](#cite_note-318)[[313]](#cite_note-319)

Murder rates in Chicago vary greatly depending on the neighborhood in question.[[314]](#cite_note-320) The neighborhoods of [Englewood](/wiki/Englewood,_Chicago "Englewood, Chicago") on the South Side, and [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Chicago "Austin, Chicago") on the West side, for example, have homicide rates that are ten times higher than other parts of the city.[[315]](#cite_note-321) Chicago has an estimated population of over 100,000 active gang members from nearly 60 factions.[[316]](#cite_note-322)[[317]](#cite_note-323) According to reports in 2013, "most of Chicago's violent crime comes from gangs trying to maintain control of drug-selling territories,"[[318]](#cite_note-bloomberg1-324) and is specifically related to the activities of the [Sinaloa Cartel](/wiki/Sinaloa_Cartel "Sinaloa Cartel"), which is active in several American cities.[[319]](#cite_note-325)

Violent crime rates vary significantly by area of the city, with more economically developed areas having low rates, but other sections have much higher rates of crime.[[318]](#cite_note-bloomberg1-324) In 2013, the violent crime rate was 910 per 100,000 people;[[320]](#cite_note-326) the murder rate was 10.4 per 100,000 – while high crime districts saw 38.9 murders, low crime districts saw 2.5 murders per 100,000.[[321]](#cite_note-327)

Chicago's long history of public [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption") regularly draws the attention of federal law enforcement and federal prosecutors.[[322]](#cite_note-328) From 2012 to 2019, 33 Chicago alderpersons were convicted on corruption charges, roughly one third of those elected in the time period. A report from the Office of the Legislative Inspector General noted that over half of Chicago's elected alderpersons took illegal campaign contributions in 2013.[[323]](#cite_note-329) Most corruption cases in Chicago are prosecuted by the [U.S. Attorney](/wiki/US_Attorney "US Attorney")'s office, as legal [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") makes most offenses punishable as a federal crime.[[324]](#cite_note-330)

## Education

### Schools and libraries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Harold_Washington_Library%2C_Chicago%2C_Illinois_%289181548762%29.jpg/250px-Harold_Washington_Library%2C_Chicago%2C_Illinois_%289181548762%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harold_Washington_Library,_Chicago,_Illinois_(9181548762).jpg)

When it was opened in 1991, the central [Harold Washington Library](/wiki/Harold_Washington_Library "Harold Washington Library") appeared in *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as the largest municipal public library building in the world.

[Chicago Public Schools](/wiki/Chicago_Public_Schools "Chicago Public Schools") (CPS) is the governing body of the [school district](/wiki/School_district "School district") that contains over 600 public elementary and high schools citywide, including several selective-admission magnet schools. There are eleven selective enrollment high schools in the Chicago Public Schools, designed to meet the needs of Chicago's most academically advanced students. These schools offer a rigorous curriculum with mainly honors and [Advanced Placement](/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") (AP) courses.[[325]](#cite_note-331) [Walter Payton College Prep High School](/wiki/Walter_Payton_College_Prep_High_School "Walter Payton College Prep High School") is ranked number one in the city of Chicago and the state of Illinois.[[326]](#cite_note-332)

Chicago high school rankings are determined by the average test scores on state achievement tests.[[327]](#cite_note-333) The district, with an enrollment exceeding 400,545 students (2013–2014 20th Day Enrollment), is the third-largest in the U.S.[[328]](#cite_note-334) In September 2012, teachers for the Chicago Teachers Union went on strike for the first time since 1987 over pay, resources, and other issues.[[329]](#cite_note-335) According to 2014 data, Chicago's "choice system", where students who test or apply and may attend one of approximately 130 public high schools, sorts students of different achievement levels into different schools (high performing, middle performing, and low performing schools).[[330]](#cite_note-336)

Chicago has a network of [Lutheran schools](/wiki/Lutheran_school "Lutheran school"),[[331]](#cite_note-337) and several private schools are run by other denominations and faiths, such as the [Ida Crown Jewish Academy](/wiki/Ida_Crown_Jewish_Academy "Ida Crown Jewish Academy") in [West Ridge](/wiki/West_Ridge,_Chicago "West Ridge, Chicago"). The [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Chicago](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Chicago "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Chicago") operates [Catholic schools](/wiki/Catholic_school "Catholic school"), including [Jesuit preparatory schools](/wiki/List_of_Jesuit_secondary_schools_in_the_United_States#Illinois "List of Jesuit secondary schools in the United States") and others. A number of private schools are completely secular. There is also the private [Chicago Academy for the Arts](/wiki/Chicago_Academy_for_the_Arts "Chicago Academy for the Arts"), a high school focused on six different artistic disciplines, and the public [Chicago High School for the Arts](/wiki/Chicago_High_School_for_the_Arts "Chicago High School for the Arts"), a high school focused on five disciplines (visual arts, theatre, musical theatre, dance, and music).[[332]](#cite_note-338)

The [Chicago Public Library](/wiki/Chicago_Public_Library "Chicago Public Library") system operates three regional libraries and 77 neighborhood branches, including the central library.[[333]](#cite_note-339)

### Colleges and universities

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of colleges and universities in Chicago](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Chicago "List of colleges and universities in Chicago").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Harper_Midway_Chicago.jpg/250px-Harper_Midway_Chicago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harper_Midway_Chicago.jpg)

The [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") campus as seen from the [Midway Plaisance](/wiki/Midway_Plaisance "Midway Plaisance")

Since the 1850s, Chicago has been a world center of higher education and research with several universities. These institutions consistently rank among the top "National Universities" in the United States, as determined by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")*.[[334]](#cite_note-340) Highly regarded universities in Chicago and the surrounding area are the University of Chicago; Northwestern University; [Illinois Institute of Technology](/wiki/Illinois_Institute_of_Technology "Illinois Institute of Technology"); [Loyola University Chicago](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago "Loyola University Chicago"); [DePaul University](/wiki/DePaul_University "DePaul University"); [Columbia College Chicago](/wiki/Columbia_College_Chicago "Columbia College Chicago") and the [University of Illinois Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_Chicago "University of Illinois Chicago"). Other notable schools include: [Chicago State University](/wiki/Chicago_State_University "Chicago State University"); the [School of the Art Institute of Chicago](/wiki/School_of_the_Art_Institute_of_Chicago "School of the Art Institute of Chicago"); [East–West University](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_University "East–West University"); [National Louis University](/wiki/National_Louis_University "National Louis University"); [North Park University](/wiki/North_Park_University "North Park University"); [Northeastern Illinois University](/wiki/Northeastern_Illinois_University "Northeastern Illinois University"); [Robert Morris University Illinois](/wiki/Robert_Morris_University_Illinois "Robert Morris University Illinois"); [Roosevelt University](/wiki/Roosevelt_University "Roosevelt University"); [Saint Xavier University](/wiki/Saint_Xavier_University "Saint Xavier University"); [Rush University](/wiki/Rush_University "Rush University"); and [Shimer College](/wiki/Shimer_College "Shimer College").[[335]](#cite_note-341)

[William Rainey Harper](/wiki/William_Rainey_Harper "William Rainey Harper"), the first president of the University of Chicago, was instrumental in the creation of the [junior college](/wiki/Junior_college "Junior college") concept, establishing nearby [Joliet Junior College](/wiki/Joliet_Junior_College "Joliet Junior College") as the first in the nation in 1901.[[336]](#cite_note-342) His legacy continues with the multiple [community colleges](/wiki/Community_college "Community college") in the Chicago proper, including the seven [City Colleges of Chicago](/wiki/City_Colleges_of_Chicago "City Colleges of Chicago"): [Richard J. Daley College](/wiki/Richard_J._Daley_College "Richard J. Daley College"), [Kennedy–King College](/wiki/Kennedy%E2%80%93King_College "Kennedy–King College"), [Malcolm X College](/wiki/Malcolm_X_College "Malcolm X College"), [Olive–Harvey College](/wiki/Olive%E2%80%93Harvey_College "Olive–Harvey College"), [Truman College](/wiki/Truman_College "Truman College"), [Harold Washington College](/wiki/Harold_Washington_College "Harold Washington College"), and [Wilbur Wright College](/wiki/Wilbur_Wright_College "Wilbur Wright College"), in addition to the privately held [MacCormac College](/wiki/MacCormac_College "MacCormac College").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Chicago also has a high concentration of post-baccalaureate institutions, graduate schools, seminaries, and theological schools, such as the [Adler School of Professional Psychology](/wiki/Adler_School_of_Professional_Psychology "Adler School of Professional Psychology"), [The Chicago School](/wiki/The_Chicago_School "The Chicago School") the [Erikson Institute](/wiki/Erikson_Institute "Erikson Institute"), [Institute for Clinical Social Work](/wiki/Institute_for_Clinical_Social_Work "Institute for Clinical Social Work"), [Lutheran School of Theology at Chicago](/wiki/Lutheran_School_of_Theology_at_Chicago "Lutheran School of Theology at Chicago"), [Catholic Theological Union](/wiki/Catholic_Theological_Union "Catholic Theological Union"), [Moody Bible Institute](/wiki/Moody_Bible_Institute "Moody Bible Institute"), and [University of Chicago Divinity School](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Divinity_School "University of Chicago Divinity School").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Media

Main article: [Media in Chicago](/wiki/Media_in_Chicago "Media in Chicago")

See also: [Chicago International Film Festival](/wiki/Chicago_International_Film_Festival "Chicago International Film Festival")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/W-G-N_Studios_Tribune_Square%2C_Chicago_%2860792%29.jpg/250px-W-G-N_Studios_Tribune_Square%2C_Chicago_%2860792%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:W-G-N_Studios_Tribune_Square,_Chicago_(60792).jpg)

[WGN](/wiki/WGN_(AM) "WGN (AM)") began in the early days of radio and developed into a multi-platform broadcaster, including a cable television super-station.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Harpo_Studio_sign.jpg/250px-Harpo_Studio_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harpo_Studio_sign.jpg)

Chicago was home of *[The Oprah Winfrey Show](/wiki/The_Oprah_Winfrey_Show "The Oprah Winfrey Show")* from 1986 until 2011, and of other Harpo Production operations until 2015.

### Television

The Chicago metropolitan area is a major media hub and the third-largest media market in the United States, after New York City and Los Angeles.[[337]](#cite_note-343) Each of the big five [U.S. television networks](/wiki/List_of_United_States_over-the-air_television_networks "List of United States over-the-air television networks"), [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") and [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW"), directly owns and operates a [high-definition television](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television") station in Chicago ([WMAQ](/wiki/WMAQ-TV "WMAQ-TV") 5, [WLS](/wiki/WLS-TV "WLS-TV") 7, [WBBM](/wiki/WBBM-TV "WBBM-TV") 2, [WFLD](/wiki/WFLD "WFLD") 32 and [WGN-TV](/wiki/WGN-TV "WGN-TV") 9, respectively). WGN is owned by the CW through a majority stake held in the network by the [Nexstar Media Group](/wiki/Nexstar_Media_Group "Nexstar Media Group"), which acquired it from its founding owner [Tribune Broadcasting](/wiki/Tribune_Broadcasting "Tribune Broadcasting") in 2019. WGN was once carried, with some programming differences, as "[WGN America](/wiki/WGN_America "WGN America")" on [cable and satellite TV](/wiki/Cable_television "Cable television") nationwide and in parts of the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). WGN America eventually became [NewsNation](/wiki/NewsNation "NewsNation") in 2021.

Chicago has been the home of several prominent talk shows, including *[The Oprah Winfrey Show](/wiki/The_Oprah_Winfrey_Show "The Oprah Winfrey Show")*, *[Steve Harvey Show](/wiki/Steve_Harvey_(talk_show) "Steve Harvey (talk show)")*, *[The Rosie Show](/wiki/The_Rosie_Show "The Rosie Show")*, *[The Jerry Springer Show](/wiki/The_Jerry_Springer_Show "The Jerry Springer Show")*, *[The Phil Donahue Show](/wiki/The_Phil_Donahue_Show "The Phil Donahue Show")*, *[The Jenny Jones Show](/wiki/The_Jenny_Jones_Show "The Jenny Jones Show")*, and more. The city has one [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") member station (its second: [WYCC](/wiki/WYCC "WYCC") 20, removed its affiliation with PBS in 2017[[338]](#cite_note-344)): [WTTW](/wiki/WTTW "WTTW") 11, producer of shows such as *[Sneak Previews](/wiki/Sneak_Previews "Sneak Previews")*, *[The Frugal Gourmet](/wiki/The_Frugal_Gourmet "The Frugal Gourmet")*, *[Lamb Chop's Play-Along](/wiki/Lamb_Chop%27s_Play-Along "Lamb Chop's Play-Along")* and *[The McLaughlin Group](/wiki/The_McLaughlin_Group "The McLaughlin Group")*.

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chicago&action=edit), *[Windy City Live](/wiki/Windy_City_Live "Windy City Live")* is Chicago's only daytime talk show, which is hosted by Val Warner and [Ryan Chiaverini](/wiki/Ryan_Chiaverini "Ryan Chiaverini") at ABC7 Studios with a live weekday audience. Since 1999, *[Judge Mathis](/wiki/Judge_Mathis "Judge Mathis")* also films his syndicated arbitration-based reality [court show](/wiki/Court_show "Court show") at the [NBC Tower](/wiki/NBC_Tower "NBC Tower"). Beginning in January 2019, *[Newsy](/wiki/Newsy "Newsy")* began producing 12 of its 14 hours of live news programming per day from its new facility in Chicago.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Television stations

Most of Chicago's television stations are owned and operated by the big television network companies.
They are:

* [WBBM-TV](/wiki/WBBM-TV "WBBM-TV") (2), owned and operated by [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS").
* [WMAQ-TV](/wiki/WMAQ-TV "WMAQ-TV") (5), owned and operated by [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC").
* [WLS-TV](/wiki/WLS-TV "WLS-TV") (7), owned and operated by [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company").
* [WGN-TV](/wiki/WGN-TV "WGN-TV") (9), a [CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW") station owned and operated by network majority owner [Nexstar Media Group](/wiki/Nexstar_Media_Group "Nexstar Media Group").
* [WTTW](/wiki/WTTW "WTTW") (11), a [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") member station owned by Window to the World Communications, Inc.
* [WCIU-TV](/wiki/WCIU-TV "WCIU-TV") (26), an independent station (with [MeTV](/wiki/MeTV "MeTV") via low-powered [WWME-CD](/wiki/WWME-CD "WWME-CD") on DT3) owned and operated by [Weigel Broadcasting](/wiki/Weigel_Broadcasting "Weigel Broadcasting").
* [WFLD](/wiki/WFLD "WFLD") (32), owned and operated by [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company").
* [WWTO-TV](/wiki/WWTO-TV "WWTO-TV") (35), owned and operated by TBN, licensed in [Naperville](/wiki/Naperville "Naperville").
* [WCPX-TV](/wiki/WCPX-TV "WCPX-TV") (38), owned and operated by [Ion Television](/wiki/Ion_Television "Ion Television").
* [WSNS-TV](/wiki/WSNS-TV "WSNS-TV") (44), owned and operated by [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo").
* [WPWR-TV](/wiki/WPWR-TV "WPWR-TV") (50), owned and operated by [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")), licensed to [Gary, Indiana](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana").
* [WYIN](/wiki/WYIN "WYIN") (56), a PBS member station owned by Northwest Indiana Public Broadcasting, Inc., licensed in Gary, Indiana.
* [WTVK](/wiki/WTVK_(TV) "WTVK (TV)") (59), an independent station owned by Venture Technologies Group, licensed in [Oswego, Illinois](/wiki/Oswego,_Illinois "Oswego, Illinois").
* [WXFT-DT](/wiki/WXFT-DT "WXFT-DT") (60), owned and operated by [Unimas](/wiki/Unimas "Unimas").
* [WJYS](/wiki/WJYS "WJYS") (62), an independent station owned by Millennial Telecommunications, Inc., licensed to [Hammond, Indiana](/wiki/Hammond,_Indiana "Hammond, Indiana").
* [WGBO-DT](/wiki/WGBO-DT "WGBO-DT") (66), owned and operated by [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision").

### Newspapers

Two major daily newspapers are published in Chicago: the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")* and the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")*, with the Tribune having the larger circulation. There are also several regional and special-interest newspapers and magazines, such as *[Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_(magazine) "Chicago (magazine)")*, the [*Dziennik Związkowy* (*Polish Daily News*)](/wiki/Dziennik_Zwi%C4%85zkowy_(Polish_Daily_News) "Dziennik Związkowy (Polish Daily News)"), *[Draugas](/wiki/Draugas "Draugas")* (the Lithuanian daily newspaper), the *[Chicago Reader](/wiki/Chicago_Reader "Chicago Reader")*, the *[SouthtownStar](/wiki/SouthtownStar "SouthtownStar")*, the *[Chicago Defender](/wiki/Chicago_Defender "Chicago Defender")*, the *[Daily Herald](/wiki/Daily_Herald_(Arlington_Heights) "Daily Herald (Arlington Heights)")*, *[Newcity](/wiki/Newcity "Newcity")*,[[339]](#cite_note-twsOctW11-345)[[340]](#cite_note-twsOctW14-346) *[StreetWise](/wiki/StreetWise "StreetWise")* and the *[Windy City Times](/wiki/Windy_City_Times "Windy City Times")*.

The entertainment and cultural magazine *[Time Out Chicago](/wiki/Time_Out_Chicago "Time Out Chicago")* and [*GRAB*](/wiki/Grab_(magazine) "Grab (magazine)") magazine are published in the city, as well as local music magazine *[Chicago Innerview](/wiki/Chicago_Innerview "Chicago Innerview")*. Chicago is the home of satirical national news outlet, *[The Onion](/wiki/The_Onion "The Onion")*, and its sister pop-culture publication, *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")*.[[341]](#cite_note-347)

"Audacy, Inc.")-owned [WBBM](/wiki/WBBM_(AM) "WBBM (AM)") and [WSCR](/wiki/WSCR "WSCR"); the [Tribune Broadcasting](/wiki/Tribune_Broadcasting "Tribune Broadcasting")-owned [WGN](/wiki/WGN_(AM) "WGN (AM)"); the [Cumulus Media](/wiki/Cumulus_Media "Cumulus Media")-owned [WLS](/wiki/WLS_(AM) "WLS (AM)"); and the [ESPN Radio](/wiki/ESPN_Radio "ESPN Radio")-owned [WMVP](/wiki/WMVP "WMVP"). "Wait Wait...Don't Tell Me!")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in Chicago](/wiki/Transportation_in_Chicago "Transportation in Chicago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Jane_M._Byrne_Interchange_%281%29_4-1-22.jpg/330px-Jane_M._Byrne_Interchange_%281%29_4-1-22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jane_M._Byrne_Interchange_(1)_4-1-22.jpg)

An aerial photo of the [Jane Byrne Interchange](/wiki/Jane_Byrne_Interchange "Jane Byrne Interchange") (2022) after reconstruction. It initially opened in the 1960s.

Chicago is a major transportation hub in the United States. It is an important component in global distribution, as it is the third-largest inter-modal port in the world after [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadigan200452-348)

The city of Chicago has a higher than average percentage of households without a car. In 2015, 26.5 percent of Chicago households were without a car, and increased slightly to 27.5 percent in 2016. The national average was 8.7 percent in 2016. Chicago averaged 1.12 cars per household in 2016, compared to a national average of 1.8.[[343]](#cite_note-349)

#### Parking

Due to Chicago's [wheel tax](/wiki/Wheel_tax "Wheel tax"),[[344]](#cite_note-350) residents of Chicago who own a vehicle are required to purchase a Chicago City Vehicle Sticker.[[345]](#cite_note-351) In established Residential Parking Zones, only local residents can purchase Zone-specific parking stickers for themselves and guests.[[346]](#cite_note-352)[[347]](#cite_note-353)

Chicago since 2009 has relinquished rights to its [public](/wiki/Commercial_area "Commercial area") street parking.[[348]](#cite_note-354) In 2008, as Chicago struggled to close a growing budget deficit, the city agreed to a 75-year, $1.16 billion deal to lease its [parking meter](/wiki/Parking_meter "Parking meter") system to an operating company created by [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley"), called [Chicago Parking Meters LLC](/wiki/Chicago_Parking_Meters_LLC "Chicago Parking Meters LLC"). Daley said the "agreement is very good news for the taxpayers of Chicago because it will provide more than $1 billion in net proceeds that can be used during this very difficult economy."[[349]](#cite_note-fail-355)

The rights of the parking ticket lease end in 2081, and since 2022 have already recouped over $1.5 billion in revenue for [Chicago Parking Meters LLC](/wiki/Chicago_Parking_Meters_LLC "Chicago Parking Meters LLC") investors.[[350]](#cite_note-356)

#### Expressways

Main article: [Roads and expressways in Chicago](/wiki/Roads_and_expressways_in_Chicago "Roads and expressways in Chicago")

Seven mainline and four auxiliary [interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") ([55](/wiki/Interstate_55_in_Illinois "Interstate 55 in Illinois"), [57](/wiki/Interstate_57 "Interstate 57"), [65](/wiki/Interstate_65_in_Indiana "Interstate 65 in Indiana") (only in Indiana), [80](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Illinois "Interstate 80 in Illinois") (also in [Indiana](/wiki/Interstate_80_in_Indiana "Interstate 80 in Indiana")), [88](/wiki/Interstate_88_(west) "Interstate 88 (west)"), [90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_Illinois "Interstate 90 in Illinois") (also in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana_Toll_Road "Indiana Toll Road")), [94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Illinois "Interstate 94 in Illinois") (also in [Indiana](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Indiana "Interstate 94 in Indiana")), [190](/wiki/Interstate_190_(Illinois) "Interstate 190 (Illinois)"), [290](/wiki/Interstate_290_(Illinois) "Interstate 290 (Illinois)"), [294](/wiki/Interstate_294 "Interstate 294"), and [355](/wiki/Interstate_355 "Interstate 355")) run through Chicago and its suburbs. Segments that link to the city center are named after influential politicians, with three of them named after former U.S. Presidents (Eisenhower, Kennedy, and Reagan) and one named after two-time Democratic candidate [Adlai Stevenson](/wiki/Adlai_Stevenson_II "Adlai Stevenson II").

The [Kennedy](/wiki/Kennedy_Expressway "Kennedy Expressway") and [Dan Ryan](/wiki/Dan_Ryan_Expressway "Dan Ryan Expressway") Expressways are the busiest state maintained routes in the entire state of Illinois.[[351]](#cite_note-357)

#### Transit systems

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/20220113_03a_CTA_Green_Line_L_near_Congress_St.jpg/250px-20220113_03a_CTA_Green_Line_L_near_Congress_St.jpg)](/wiki/File:20220113_03a_CTA_Green_Line_L_near_Congress_St.jpg)

The [CTA](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority") operates the [Chicago "L"](/wiki/Chicago_%22L%22 "Chicago \"L\"")

The [Regional Transportation Authority](/wiki/Regional_Transportation_Authority_(Illinois) "Regional Transportation Authority (Illinois)") (RTA) coordinates the operation of the three service boards: CTA, Metra, and Pace.

* The [Chicago Transit Authority](/wiki/Chicago_Transit_Authority "Chicago Transit Authority") (CTA) handles public transportation in the City of Chicago and a few adjacent suburbs outside of the Chicago city limits. The CTA operates an extensive network of buses and a [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") elevated and subway system known as the [Chicago "L"](/wiki/Chicago_%22L%22 "Chicago \"L\"") (or just the "L") (short for "elevated"), with lines designated by colors. These rapid transit lines also serve both [Midway](/wiki/Chicago_Midway_International_Airport "Chicago Midway International Airport") and O'Hare Airports. The CTA's rail lines consist of the [Red](/wiki/Red_Line_(CTA) "Red Line (CTA)"), [Blue](/wiki/Blue_Line_(CTA) "Blue Line (CTA)"), [Green](/wiki/Green_Line_(CTA) "Green Line (CTA)"), [Orange](/wiki/Orange_Line_(CTA) "Orange Line (CTA)"), [Brown](/wiki/Brown_Line_(CTA) "Brown Line (CTA)"), [Purple](/wiki/Purple_Line_(CTA) "Purple Line (CTA)"), [Pink](/wiki/Pink_Line_(CTA) "Pink Line (CTA)"), and [Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_Line_(CTA) "Yellow Line (CTA)") lines. The Red and Blue lines both offer 24‑hour service which makes Chicago one of a handful of cities around the world (and one of three in the United States, the other are New York City and Philadelphia) to offer rail service 24 hours a day, every day of the year, within the city's limits.
* [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra"), the nation's second-most used passenger regional rail network, operates an 11-line [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") service in Chicago and throughout the Chicago suburbs. The [Metra Electric District](/wiki/Metra_Electric_District "Metra Electric District") shares its trackage with Northern Indiana Commuter Transportation District (NICTD)'s [South Shore Line](/wiki/South_Shore_Line "South Shore Line"), which provides commuter service between [South Bend](/wiki/South_Bend,_Indiana "South Bend, Indiana") and Chicago.
* [Pace](/wiki/Pace_Suburban_Bus "Pace Suburban Bus") provides bus and [paratransit](/wiki/Paratransit "Paratransit") service in over 200 surrounding suburbs with some extensions into the city as well. A 2005 study found that one quarter of commuters used public transit.[[352]](#cite_note-358)

[Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") provides inter-city bus service to and from the city at the [Chicago Bus Station](/wiki/Chicago_Bus_Station "Chicago Bus Station"), and Chicago is also the hub for the Midwest network of [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)").

Beginning in June 2026, the RTA will be replaced by the Northern Illinois Transit Authority (NITA).[[353]](#cite_note-359)

#### Passenger rail

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Chicago_Union_Station.jpg/250px-Chicago_Union_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_Union_Station.jpg)

[Chicago Union Station](/wiki/Chicago_Union_Station "Chicago Union Station") is [Amtrak's](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") hub for both national long-distance trains and inter-city trains in the [Midwest](/wiki/Amtrak_Midwest "Amtrak Midwest"). It's also served by six [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra") commuter lines.

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") long distance and [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") services originate from [Chicago Union Station](/wiki/Chicago_Union_Station "Chicago Union Station").[[354]](#cite_note-360) Chicago is one of the largest hubs of passenger rail service in the nation.[[355]](#cite_note-361) The services terminate in [Port Huron](/wiki/Port_Huron,_Michigan "Port Huron, Michigan"), [St. Paul](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota"), the San Francisco Area, New York City, New Orleans, [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [Carbondale](/wiki/Carbondale,_Illinois "Carbondale, Illinois"), [Quincy](/wiki/Quincy,_Illinois "Quincy, Illinois"), Boston, [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), [Grand Rapids](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio "San Antonio"), and [Pontiac, Michigan](/wiki/Pontiac,_Michigan "Pontiac, Michigan"). Future service will terminate at [Moline](/wiki/Moline,_Illinois "Moline, Illinois"). An attempt was made in the early 20th century to link Chicago with New York City via the [Chicago – New York Electric Air Line Railroad](/wiki/Chicago_%E2%80%93_New_York_Electric_Air_Line_Railroad "Chicago – New York Electric Air Line Railroad"). Parts of this were built, but it was never completed.

Chicago has a total of four active rail terminals: [Chicago Union Station](/wiki/Chicago_Union_Station "Chicago Union Station"), [Ogilvie Transportation Center](/wiki/Ogilvie_Transportation_Center "Ogilvie Transportation Center"), [LaSalle Street Station](/wiki/LaSalle_Street_Station "LaSalle Street Station"), and [Millennium Station](/wiki/Millennium_Station "Millennium Station"). Amtrak only serves Union Station, while Metra serves all four stations. The South Shore Line uses Millennium Station as its Chicago terminal.[[356]](#cite_note-362)

#### Bicycle and scooter sharing systems

In July 2013, the [bicycle-sharing system](/wiki/Bicycle-sharing_system "Bicycle-sharing system") [Divvy](/wiki/Divvy "Divvy") was launched with 750 bikes and 75 docking stations.[[357]](#cite_note-363) It is operated by [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft") for the [Chicago Department of Transportation](/wiki/Chicago_Department_of_Transportation "Chicago Department of Transportation").[[358]](#cite_note-364) As of July 2019, Divvy operated 5800 bicycles at 608 stations, covering almost all of the city, excluding [Pullman](/wiki/Pullman,_Chicago "Pullman, Chicago"), Rosedale, [Beverly](/wiki/Beverly,_Chicago "Beverly, Chicago"), [Belmont Cragin](/wiki/Belmont_Cragin,_Chicago "Belmont Cragin, Chicago") and [Edison Park](/wiki/Edison_Park,_Chicago "Edison Park, Chicago").[[359]](#cite_note-365)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/20140802_38_Divvy_Bikes_%2820836684981%29.jpg/250px-20140802_38_Divvy_Bikes_%2820836684981%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:20140802_38_Divvy_Bikes_(20836684981).jpg)

[Divvy](/wiki/Divvy "Divvy") bikes

In May 2019, The City of Chicago announced its Chicago's Electric Shared Scooter Pilot Program, scheduled to run from June 15 to October 15.[[360]](#cite_note-366) The program started on June 15 with 10 different scooter companies, including scooter sharing market leaders [Bird](/wiki/Bird_Global "Bird Global"), [Jump](/wiki/Jump_(transportation_company) "Jump (transportation company)"), [Lime](/wiki/Lime_(transportation_company) "Lime (transportation company)") and [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft").[[361]](#cite_note-367) Each company was allowed to bring 250 [electric scooters](/wiki/Electric_motorcycles_and_scooters "Electric motorcycles and scooters"), although both Bird and Lime claimed that they experienced a higher demand for their scooters.[[362]](#cite_note-368) The program ended on October 15, with nearly 800,000 rides taken.[[363]](#cite_note-369)

#### Freight rail

Chicago is the largest hub in the railroad industry.[[364]](#cite_note-370) All five [Class I railroads](/wiki/Class_I_railroad "Class I railroad") meet in Chicago. As of 2002[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chicago&action=edit), severe freight train congestion caused trains to take as long to get through the Chicago region as it took to get there from the West Coast of the country (about 2 days).[[365]](#cite_note-371) According to U.S. Department of Transportation, the volume of imported and exported goods transported via rail to, from, or through Chicago is forecast to increase nearly 150 percent between 2010 and 2040.[[366]](#cite_note-372) CREATE, the [Chicago Region Environmental and Transportation Efficiency Program](/wiki/Chicago_Region_Environmental_and_Transportation_Efficiency_Program "Chicago Region Environmental and Transportation Efficiency Program"), comprises about 70 programs, including crossovers, overpasses and underpasses, that intend to significantly improve the speed of freight movements in the Chicago area.[[367]](#cite_note-373)

#### Airports

Further information: [Transportation in Chicago § Airports](/wiki/Transportation_in_Chicago#Airports "Transportation in Chicago")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Chicago_O%27Hare_International_Airport.jpg/330px-Chicago_O%27Hare_International_Airport.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_O%27Hare_International_Airport.jpg)

[O'Hare International Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport")

Chicago is served by [O'Hare International Airport](/wiki/O%27Hare_International_Airport "O'Hare International Airport"), the world's busiest airport measured by airline operations,[[368]](#cite_note-374) on the far Northwest Side, and [Midway International Airport](/wiki/Midway_International_Airport "Midway International Airport") on the Southwest Side. In 2005, O'Hare was the world's busiest airport by aircraft movements and the second-busiest by total passenger traffic.[[369]](#cite_note-375) Both O'Hare and Midway are owned and operated by the City of Chicago.[[370]](#cite_note-376)

[Gary/Chicago International Airport](/wiki/Gary/Chicago_International_Airport "Gary/Chicago International Airport") and [Chicago Rockford International Airport](/wiki/Chicago_Rockford_International_Airport "Chicago Rockford International Airport"), located in [Gary, Indiana](/wiki/Gary,_Indiana "Gary, Indiana") and [Rockford, Illinois](/wiki/Rockford,_Illinois "Rockford, Illinois"), respectively, can serve as alternative Chicago area airports, however they do not offer as many commercial flights as O'Hare and Midway. In recent years the state of Illinois has been leaning towards [building an entirely new airport](/wiki/Proposed_Chicago_south_suburban_airport "Proposed Chicago south suburban airport") in the Illinois suburbs of Chicago.[[371]](#cite_note-377) The City of Chicago is the world headquarters for [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines"), the world's third-largest airline.

#### Port authority

Main article: [Port of Chicago](/wiki/Port_of_Chicago "Port of Chicago")

The Port of Chicago consists of several major port facilities within the city of Chicago operated by the Illinois International Port District (formerly known as the Chicago Regional Port District). The central element of the Port District, Calumet Harbor, is maintained by the [U.S. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/U.S._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "U.S. Army Corps of Engineers").[[372]](#cite_note-378)

* Iroquois Landing Lakefront Terminal: at the mouth of the Calumet River, it includes 100 acres (0.40 km2) of warehouses and facilities on Lake Michigan with over 780,000 square meters (8,400,000 ft2) of storage.
* Lake Calumet terminal: located at the union of the [Grand Calumet River](/wiki/Grand_Calumet_River "Grand Calumet River") and [Little Calumet River](/wiki/Little_Calumet_River "Little Calumet River") 6 miles (9.7 km) inland from Lake Michigan. Includes three transit sheds totaling over 29,000 square meters (310,000 ft2) adjacent to over 900 linear meters (3,000 linear feet) of ship and barge berthing.
* Grain (14 million bushels) and bulk liquid (800,000 barrels) storage facilities along [Lake Calumet](/wiki/Lake_Calumet "Lake Calumet").
* The Illinois International Port district also operates [Foreign trade zone](/wiki/Foreign_trade_zones_of_the_United_States "Foreign trade zones of the United States") No. 22, which extends 60 miles (97 km) from Chicago's city limits.

### Utilities

Electricity for most of [northern Illinois](/wiki/Northern_Illinois "Northern Illinois") is provided by [Commonwealth Edison](/wiki/Commonwealth_Edison "Commonwealth Edison"), also known as ComEd. Their service territory borders [Iroquois County](/wiki/Iroquois_County,_Illinois "Iroquois County, Illinois") to the south, the [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") border to the north, the [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") border to the west and the [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") border to the east. In northern Illinois, ComEd (a division of [Exelon](/wiki/Exelon "Exelon")) operates the greatest number of nuclear generating plants in any U.S. state. Because of this, ComEd reports indicate that Chicago receives about 75% of its electricity from nuclear power. Recently, the city began installing wind turbines on government buildings to promote renewable energy.[[373]](#cite_note-379)[[374]](#cite_note-380)[[375]](#cite_note-381)

Natural gas is provided by Peoples Gas, a subsidiary of [Integrys Energy Group](/wiki/Integrys_Energy_Group "Integrys Energy Group"), which is headquartered in Chicago.

Domestic and industrial waste was once incinerated but it is now [landfilled](/wiki/Landfill "Landfill"), mainly in the [Calumet area](/wiki/Lake_Calumet "Lake Calumet"). From 1995 to 2008, the city had a [blue bag](/wiki/Blue_bag "Blue bag") program to divert recyclable refuse from landfills.[[376]](#cite_note-382) Because of low participation in the blue bag programs, the city began a pilot program for blue bin recycling like other cities. This proved successful and blue bins were rolled out across the city.[[377]](#cite_note-383)

### Health systems

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Prentice_Chicago_060816.jpg/250px-Prentice_Chicago_060816.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prentice_Chicago_060816.jpg)

Prentice Women's Hospital on the [Northwestern Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Northwestern_Memorial_Hospital "Northwestern Memorial Hospital") Downtown Campus

The [Illinois Medical District](/wiki/Illinois_Medical_District "Illinois Medical District") is on the Near West Side. It includes [Rush University Medical Center](/wiki/Rush_University_Medical_Center "Rush University Medical Center"), ranked as the second best hospital in the Chicago metropolitan area by *U.S. News & World Report* for 2014–16, the [University of Illinois Medical Center at Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_College_of_Medicine "University of Illinois College of Medicine"), Jesse Brown VA Hospital, and [John H. Stroger Jr. Hospital of Cook County](/wiki/John_H._Stroger_Jr._Hospital_of_Cook_County "John H. Stroger Jr. Hospital of Cook County"), one of the busiest trauma centers in the nation.[[378]](#cite_note-384)

Two of the country's premier academic medical centers, [Northwestern Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Northwestern_Memorial_Hospital "Northwestern Memorial Hospital") and the [University of Chicago Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Medical_Center "University of Chicago Medical Center"), reside in Chicago. The Chicago campus of Northwestern University includes the [Feinberg School of Medicine](/wiki/Feinberg_School_of_Medicine "Feinberg School of Medicine"); Northwestern Memorial Hospital, which was ranked as the best hospital in the Chicago metropolitan area by *U.S. News & World Report* for 2017–18;[[379]](#cite_note-385) the [Shirley Ryan AbilityLab](/wiki/Shirley_Ryan_AbilityLab "Shirley Ryan AbilityLab") (formerly named the Rehabilitation Institute of Chicago), which was ranked the best U.S. rehabilitation hospital by *U.S. News & World Report*;[[380]](#cite_note-386) the new [Prentice Women's Hospital](/wiki/List_of_Northwestern_University_buildings#Prentice_Women's_Hospital "List of Northwestern University buildings"); and Ann & Robert H. Lurie Children's Hospital of Chicago.

The [University of Illinois College of Medicine](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_College_of_Medicine "University of Illinois College of Medicine") at UIC is the second-largest medical school in the United States (2,600 students, including those at campuses in Peoria, Rockford and [Urbana–Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana%E2%80%93Champaign "University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign")).[[381]](#cite_note-387)

In addition, the [Chicago Medical School](/wiki/Chicago_Medical_School "Chicago Medical School") and Loyola University Chicago's [Stritch School of Medicine](/wiki/Stritch_School_of_Medicine "Stritch School of Medicine") are located in the suburbs of [North Chicago](/wiki/North_Chicago,_Illinois "North Chicago, Illinois") and [Maywood](/wiki/Maywood,_Illinois "Maywood, Illinois"), respectively. The [Midwestern University](/wiki/Midwestern_University "Midwestern University") Chicago College of [Osteopathic Medicine](/wiki/Osteopathic_medicine_in_the_United_States "Osteopathic medicine in the United States") is in [Downers Grove](/wiki/Downers_Grove,_Illinois "Downers Grove, Illinois").

The [American Medical Association](/wiki/American_Medical_Association "American Medical Association"), [Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education](/wiki/Accreditation_Council_for_Graduate_Medical_Education "Accreditation Council for Graduate Medical Education"), [Accreditation Council for Continuing Medical Education](/wiki/Accreditation_Council_for_Continuing_Medical_Education "Accreditation Council for Continuing Medical Education"), [American Osteopathic Association](/wiki/American_Osteopathic_Association "American Osteopathic Association"), [American Dental Association](/wiki/American_Dental_Association "American Dental Association"), [Academy of General Dentistry](/wiki/Academy_of_General_Dentistry "Academy of General Dentistry"), [Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics](/wiki/Academy_of_Nutrition_and_Dietetics "Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics"), [American Association of Nurse Anesthetists](/wiki/American_Association_of_Nurse_Anesthetists "American Association of Nurse Anesthetists"), [American College of Surgeons](/wiki/American_College_of_Surgeons "American College of Surgeons"), [American Society for Clinical Pathology](/wiki/American_Society_for_Clinical_Pathology "American Society for Clinical Pathology"), [American College of Healthcare Executives](/wiki/American_College_of_Healthcare_Executives "American College of Healthcare Executives"), the [American Hospital Association](/wiki/American_Hospital_Association "American Hospital Association"), and [Blue Cross and Blue Shield Association](/wiki/Blue_Cross_and_Blue_Shield_Association "Blue Cross and Blue Shield Association") are all based in Chicago.
